Arbi Berisha – cousin of one of the victims, Faton Morina, 32 – told Blick he received a frantic call from Faton at around 6.30pm telling him to come to the scene as quickly as possible.

Faton suffered injuries to his hands, nose, and legs, said Arbi.

Although he survived the ordeal, Arbi added: “He is currently very traumatized.”

Faton then described how the terrifying incident unfolded.



He said the suspect was sitting directly in front of him on the bus.

After the man set himself on fire, the vehicle came to a stop and Faton tried to get off but the doors were closed.

Moments later, he heard a noise behind him and managed to escape through the rear of the bus.

The Swiss President addressed the shocking incident on social media.

“It distresses and saddens me that once again people in Switzerland have lost their lives in a severe fire,” the President said.



“The circumstances are being investigated.

“To the relatives of the deceased from Kerzers, I extend my condolences.

“And I think of the injured and the rescue workers.”

It remains unclear why so many passengers were unable to escape the burnning bus.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Police are now investigating video footage from inside the vehicle to ascertain whether the doors prevented others from making it out alive.



In the meantime, a specialist care team and hotline, as well as a memorial have been set up for those affected by harrowing episode.

News.title: The tiny Gulf island that could seal fate of Iran – why Trump is holding back on blitz that would rock the world
News.content:
BARREN and only five miles long, sun- scorched Kharg Island could become the battleground where Donald Trump declares victory over Iran.

Few could point to it on a map yet Trump has long known the tiny coral ­outcrop adrift in the Persian Gulf is the Islamic Republic’s Achilles’ heel.





Back in 1988 when the 47th US ­President was still a wise-cracking New York property mogul, he told a reporter he’d win back “respect” for his nation if he ever made it to the White House.

With Iran already a bitter US foe, Trump said: “They’ve been beating us psychologically, making us look a bunch of fools. One bullet shot at one of our men or ships, and I’d do a number on Kharg Island. I’d go in and take it.”

Now speculation is growing that the Commander-In-Chief and his top brass are mulling over fulfilling that strategy, four decades on.

For whoever controls Kharg Island controls Iran.

Nine out of ten barrels of oil that the petro-state produces are shipped from this scrap of land, which is half the size of New York’s Manhattan Island.

Fossil fuel exports make up around 40 per cent of Iran’s budget, with profits funding the mullahs’ waves of kamikaze Shahed drones and oppressive security apparatus.

So why haven’t US Tomahawk missiles already blown the oil terminals there to kingdom come?

Trump’s Operation Epic Fury has already taken out Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and swathes of Iran’s other high-ranking leadership, as well as missile launchers, air defences and its navy.



Yet, Kharg Island — 15 miles off Iran’s coast in the northern stretches of the Gulf — remains untouched.

US thinking is that destroying the island would risk sending further shock waves through energy markets and weaken any future Iranian government, if the ayatollahs are overthrown.

Yet Iran’s throttling of the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lanes may force Trump’s hand.

From a vantage point on Jebel Ali Beach, I was able to view the sprawling oil refinery, container port and natural gas terminal on the outskirts of Dubai.

It should have fleets of tankers queueing to transport the black gold across the globe.



Yet it is currently empty of ships.

The MarineTraffic app shows around 200 tankers anchored in the Gulf waters.

Desperately fighting for its very existence, Iran’s regime has the nearby Strait of Hormuz in a stranglehold.

Some 21 miles across at its narrowest point, a fifth of the world’s oil and gas passes through this ocean chokepoint.

Bloodied but not beaten, Iran has attacked at least 13 commercial ships attempting to pass through the Strait.



Yesterday three tankers were hit by “unknown projectiles”, including the Thailand-flagged Mayuree Naree, which billowed black smoke after being targeted off the coast of Oman.

A day earlier it emerged Iran has laid dozens of mines in the strait in recent days and has the capability to sow hundreds more, according to an intelligence source.

Little wonder then that the shipping lanes across the Gulf waters from where I’m standing in Jebel Ali are largely free of through traffic.



Trump has vowed, “death, fire and fury will reign upon” Iran if it doesn’t allow tankers laden with oil to pass through the Strait.

On Tuesday the US military released declassified footage of air attacks in which it said 16 mine-laying vessels were “eliminated”.

At least for now, the oil tankers remain anchored.

The simple laws of supply and demand mean oil prices surged as high as $120 a barrel before recovering some ground.

Yesterday 32 countries agreed the largest ever release of oil reserves in an attempt to stabilise prices.



Yet energy markets remain highly volatile.

If the Strait remains closed for a long period, the economic blowback will be felt in the UK and across the globe.

Stock markets will plunge while inflation and interest rates rise. Prices at the petrol pumps will spike.

The United Arab Emirates and the other Gulf states — whose gleaming cities in the desert were built on petrol dollars — know only too well what will happen if the oil flow is stifled.

Qatar’s energy minister, Saad al-Kaabi, has warned that if this war continues, it could “bring down the economies of the world”.



It’s why an attack on Kharg Island may be on the table.

Trump has signalled that the US bombing campaign will move beyond military and nuclear targets.

However, the White House declined to comment on its strategy around Kharg Island.

Others are more openly bullish.

Israeli opposition leader Yair Lapid called for his nation’s military to “destroy all of Iran’s oilfields and energy industry on Kharg Island . . . that is what will cripple Iran’s economy and topple the regime”.



Yet, one energy executive in the region said: “My worry is that the US likes the idea of a hit on Kharg — then the gloves are off.”

Richard Nephew, a former US deputy special envoy for Iran, said of targeting Kharg: “The US and Israel are aware that if you hit it, you risk Iran really targeting the oil infrastructure of Gulf states, and if regime change is successful it makes the successor government extremely weak.

“This does not mean it won’t be targeted, but it explains why it hasn’t yet.”

It means a boots-on-the-ground invasion by US forces — keeping the oil terminals intact — may be the preferred option.



Petras Katinas, a research fellow in climate, energy and defence at the Royal United Services Institute, said: “Seizing the island would cut off Iran’s oil lifeline, which is crucial for the regime.

“Of course, with shipping via the Strait of Hormuz now stopped, Iran cannot sell its oil anyway but, looking ahead, seizure would give the US leverage during negotiations, no matter which regime is in power after the military operation ends.”

In a further twist, China buys more than 80 per cent of all Iran’s oil exports.

International politics expert Guy Laron from the Hebrew University of Jerusalem wrote: “Having the US control the Persian Gulf from which China imports roughly half its crude and a third of its gas is a strategic catastrophe for Beijing.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

This little rock-strewn island could prove an irresistible prize for Trump.



Seizing Kharg would see him control the most strategic piece of Iranian soil — and allow him to declare his war with the ayatollahs over.

News.title: Boy, 16, stabbed in Aldi supermarket in broad daylight ‘after being chased by masked men’
News.content:
SUPERMARKET ATTACK Boy, 16, stabbed in Aldi supermarket in broad daylight ‘after being chased by masked men’

A TEEN boy was stabbed in a packed Aldi supermarket after reportedly being chased through the aisles by masked men.

The 16-year-old was allegedly hunted through the store by four men wearing balaclavas before being knifed during the terrifying broad-daylight attack in Toulouse.





The horror unfolded at around 1.15pm on Wednesday at an Aldi branch in the Empalot district as shoppers were browsing for food.

Witnesses reportedly saw the teen and a friend being chased through the supermarket aisles by a group of men covering their faces.

A police source told local outlet Actu Toulouse: “He indicated that three of them were wearing balaclavas and were armed with knives.”

The boy was allegedly stabbed twice, suffering wounds to his hip and shoulder blade, according to local media.



Emergency crews rushed to the scene and treated the teen inside the store before taking him to Rangueil Hospital.

Firefighters and paramedics reportedly said his injuries were not considered life-threatening.

The masked attackers had already fled the scene by the time emergency services arrived and police have launched a search to track them down.

The shocking violence reportedly comes less than three months after a similar incident at the same Aldi.



On December 28 last year, a man was allegedly attacked outside the supermarket and forced to hide in the aisles after being struck repeatedly on the head.

But on that occasion the violence reportedly began outside before spilling into the busy store, leaving stunned shoppers watching the chaos unfold.

It comes after a Brit tourist collapsed inside a 7-Eleven in Thailand after being stabbed while buying water.

The 22-year-old holidaymaker was knifed in the thigh during a row with a local shopper in the early hours of February 13 in Pattaya.

He had been purchasing a bottle of water when a 23-year-old is said to have barged into him inside the store.



The pair began arguing before the Thai man allegedly pulled out a knife and plunged it into the Brit’s right thigh.

Terrified shoppers fled as blood spewed from his leg.

He collapsed on the floor while quick-thinking assistants stemmed the bleeding and bystanders cornered the attacker in a nearby aisle.

The Thai suspect was detained inside the shop by nearby police officers and paramedics gave the Brit first aid at the scene.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

He was lying unconscious on the floor as blood streamed from his leg, before being wheeled out into an ambulance.



Cops said the tourist later discharged himself from hospital and walked back to Pattaya’s notorious strip of bars.

News.title: Donald Trump rally speech interrupted by medical emergency - 'we need a medic!'
News.content:
Donald Trump rally speech interrupted by medical emergency - 'we need a medic!'

Oblivious to what was unfolding, Trump continued his speech - in which he was at the time attacking California Governor Gavin Newsom - before being alerted to the situation by people shouting "medic, medic"

View Image Trump supporters cried out for a medic, interrupting Trump's speech

Donald Trump's rally speech in Kentucky was interrupted tonight, as a medical emergency unfolded immediately behind the US President.

‌



An elderly Trump supporter standing on the podium behind Trump began to sway and looked unwell. Other supporters moved out of the way to allow her to brace herself on the barrier - but after a few moments she appeared to lose consciousness and was helped to the floor.

‌



Oblivious to what was unfolding behind him, Trump continued his speech - in which he was at the time attacking California Governor Gavin Newsom - before being alerted to the situation by people shouting "medic, medic."

‌



Trump turned and saw what was happening and said: "Is there a doctor in the house please?" He told those rushing to the fan's aide to "take your time....we have all the time. These people are great people. We have all the time."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

As medics arrived to assist, Trump added: "First responders are incredible, by the way." He then said to the crowd: "Wanna hear a song? Let's see if the people backstage are listening to me. How about "Ave Maria?"

‌



TV doctor-turned Trump administration health official Dr Mehmet Oz was among those assisting the woman who collapsed.

"It's Dr Oz, can you believe it?" Trump said as the crowd applauded. "Dr Oz! Wow, he's a good doctor."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



Trump is visiting Kentucky on his "affordability tour". In November, the White House announced that Trump would travel the country more frequently in an attempt to stave off a drubbing in this year's midterm elections by showing he’s taking America's cost of living crisis seriously.

Up to the point of interruption, he had spent most of the speech talking about the war in Iran, which has sent US petrol prices skyrocketing.

The US national average petrol price as of today is $3.578 a gallon - nearly 60 cents higher than it was a month ago, according to the US motoring and travel organisation AAA.

Article continues below

He also gave several shout-outs to Youtuber-turned mid-level boxer Jake Paul, who is in the audience. Paul is a Trump supporter.

News.title: Trump claims Iran war will end 'soon' because there is 'nothing left to target'
News.content:
Trump claims Iran war will end 'soon' because there is 'nothing left to target'

Donald Trump has offered shifting war goals since he and his Israeli counterpart Benjamin Netanyahu ordered strikes on the Iranian capital Tehran earlier this month

View 3 Images Donald Trump said he expected the war in Iran to end "soon" (Image: Getty Images)

Donald Trump has claimed the war in Iran will end "soon" as there is apparently "practically nothing left to target."

‌



The US President appears to have signalled an off ramp to exit its intervention in Iran after it unleashed air strikes alongside Israel earlier this month. Trump said "the war is going great." He added: "We are way ahead of the timetable. We have done more damage than we thought possible, even in the original six-week period."

‌



Trump later continued: "Any time I want it to end, it will end." The claim will add to the mixed messaging Trump has made of the longevity of the war.

‌



View 3 Images Trump has offered different war goals following his intervention in Iran (Image: Getty Images)

The comments Trump made to outlet Axios indicates Trump is keen to tell the American people that the country will not get bogged down in another lengthy war in the Middle East. Trump previously said of Iran: “They are paying for 47 years of death and destruction they caused. This is payback. They will not get off that easy."

The Trump administration has offered multiple war aims including stopping Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, protecting the US and Europe from an imminent threat and regime change. Trump's shifting justifications for the war have been met with scepticism among a significant proportion of his base, many of whom voted Republican in 2024 in part over his promise not to drag the US into new wars.

‌



Trump marched into the war without building a case for it and is now dealing with rising oil prices as suppliers flag concerns about supplies coming through the Strait of Hormuz. Trump today said the fluctuations in oil prices a "matter of war."

View 3 Images Israel and the US have launched strikes against Iran and its proxies (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

The US President has also wavered over whether to call the military operation a war. When speaking to a journalist about whether it was a war or an excursion, Trump said: "Well, it's both."

Article continues below

"It's an excursion that will keep us out of a war...for them it's a war, for us it's turned out to be easier than we thought."

The US has claimed Iran's missile attacks have been reduced by some 90 per cent since the tart of the war nearly 11 days ago. It is believed Iran may have had as many as 100,000 deadly drones at the beginning of the war, 5,000 short range missiles and a possible 2,500 ballistic missiles.

Sixty per cent of its launchers have been hit by air-strikes and it is believed 2,410 ballistic missiles remain, whilst Iran has up to 200 missile launchers left.

News.title: Horror lorry crash sees tyres fly '30ft in air' as vehicle erupts into flames
News.content:
A horror two vehicle crash saw a lorry burst into flames on a major road, causing tyres to fly "30 feet into air".

Emergency services raced to A1 between the Thistly Cross roundabout and Haddington in East Lothian, Scotland, at around 3.50pm this afternoon. The road was immediately sealed off with drivers urged to avoid the area. Shocking footage taken at the crash site captured a lorry covered in flames, with thick plumes of smoke pouring out of the vehicle, Edinburgh Live reports.

A witness at the scene, Mark Innes, said: "It carried forward with the momentum, driving along the motorway and it probably almost instantly burst into flames. It was terrifying. It was honestly like something out of a film. It's good that everyone is okay.

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

"I got my van into a safe position, and by that point that was a few bystanders and other drivers helping. By the time I ran over the driver was out, he was obviously very shaken and panicked. The lorry was exploding. There were tyres blowing thirty feet into the air and crashing down, going into the nearby bushes and setting them on fire."

Police Scotland said: "The A1 is closed northbound between the Thistly Cross roundabout and the Haddington turn off following a two-vehicle crash reported to police around 3.50pm on Wednesday, 11 March. Road users are advised to avoid the area."

Traffic Scotland earlier told drivers to "expect delays" and "use alternative routes" as the A1 was closed northbound at Haddington at around 4pm.

News.title: Moment Magaluf rape gang's teen Brit victim rescued - and 'wolf pack' caught
News.content:
Moment Magaluf rape gang's teen Brit victim rescued - and 'wolf pack' caught

The 18-year-old victim was attacked by a group of men - known as the "wolf pack" - who were sentenced to a combined 73 years after they took a plea bargain to avoid more than 150 years if convicted

View 5 Images The eight men have been sentenced to multiple years in prison (Image: SOLARPIX.COM/J.Bastida)

A hotel security guard seized the moment to rescue a British teenage gang-rape victim with a group, dubbed the "wolf pack," being arrested over the horror sex attack.

‌



The seven French men and one Swiss national, known as the "wolf pack" accepted prison sentences totalling just over 73 years after they struck a plea bargain deal with prosecutors just days before their trial over the rape was due to begin. Before deciding to plead guilty, they had been warned they faced being jailed for more than 150 years if convicted.

‌



They were arrested after a British 18-year-old was gang-raped at the BH Mallorca Resort in Magaluf. The thugs recorded the rape with some in the group whipping and slapping her as others chanted sick encouragement. The footage recorded by the men proved key in their arrests and the prosecution that followed.

‌



View 5 Images The eight men took a plea deal after the woman was gang raped (Image: SOLARPIX.COM)

Sickening reality of party town Magaluf from gang rape to 'manosphere' terror READ MORE :

A security guard at the hotel where the incident happened came to the rescue of the British teen and found her sobbing on the floor in the lobby. He had just arrived for his morning shift and described the police response at the time was said to have been very quick and efficient.

Bruises were found on the victim's arm during a medical examination and are believed to have been caused when she was held down. She was also found to have suffered a cut to her chest.

‌



The eight men who were convicted have since been named and have been ordered to compensate their victim with more than €100,000 (£86,313). French tourists Khalil Abderrahmen Mejbri and Romain Charles Michel Galatioto raped their victim, who had been drinking heavily and "fell into a state of unconscious," the court heard.

The sickening footage recorded by the men, who came from different groups and didn’t all know each other before they targeted their victim, proved key in their arrests and subsequent successful prosecution

French national Anthony Guy Gerard Wengler and Swiss national Haiaiel Ngandjeu Sjamen Tchatchou raped her at "least once," as well as Thomas Eric Coretin Wisniewski who is also from France. Tchatchou and Wengler spat at the victim and and whipped her along with Mejbri after Frenchman Satilmis Sahan slapped her.

‌



View 5 Images The men have been ordered to compensate the victim (Image: SOLARPIX.COM)

French national Anthony Auque, who was holidaying with Wengler, and fellow Frenchman Mouhamadou Wade watched on while the 30-minute gang rape occurred, surrounding the victim along with the rest of the group chanting sick encouragement during the attack.

The plea agreement all eight men accepted and signed, says: “Each one of the defendants with their mobile phones, during the incidents previously described, recorded several videos.

‌



"Anthony Auque recorded with his mobile phone 14 videos lasting 170 seconds in total; Khalil Abderrahmen Mejbri recorded with his mobile phone five videos lasting 142 seconds; Mohamadou Wade recorded with his mobile phone two videos lasting a total of 26 seconds and took a photograph; and Anthony Guy Gerard Wengler; Satilmis Sahan; Romain Charles Michel Galatioto; Thomas Eric Coretin Wisniewski; and Lucas Haiaiel Ngandjeu Djamen Tchatchou did the same on at least one occasion.

View 5 Images The rape happened at a Magaluf hotel (Image: SOLARPIX.COM)

"Boasting about the actions taken and with the clear intention of humiliating and violating his victim, Mejbri and Wade sent and shared the content of their recordings through the Snapchat app.

‌



"Those recordings were taken during the time in which their victim was semi-conscious or unconscious so that she was unable to consent to it." Mejbri and Wisniewski accepted a nine-year prison sentence for a crime of “sexual assault with carnal access,” a severe form of sexual crime involving non-consensual sexual penetration of the victim.

Wengler, Tchatchou and Galatioto were handed 11-year jail sentences after admitting the same offence. Sahan admitted a crime of “sexual assault without penetration” for which he was given a four-year prison sentence.

View 5 Images The hotel security found the victim in the lobby (Image: SOLARPIX.COM/J.Bastida)

‌



All eight men, the five self-confessed sexual offenders as well as Auque and Wade who admitted to filming their victim being targeted, were each handed two year three month prison sentences after admitting a crime of violation of privacy relating to the recordings they made on their mobile phones.

Sahan, Auque and Wade had already been granted bail before the scheduled start of yesterday’s trial and remain on bail. At least two of them are unlikely to be hauled back to prison because of time they served on remand behind bars before they were granted bail around nine months ago.

The other five remain in prison pending a further court hearing next month where a judge is set to be asked by their defence lawyers to agree to kick them out of Spain once they have served part of their jail time.

Article continues below

The teenage British victim was not required to give evidence beyond the witness statement she had already given under oath to investigators.

News.title: Inside Strictly bloodbath from 'cunning BBC plan to how pros were picked for axe'
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Inside Strictly bloodbath from 'cunning BBC plan to how pros were picked for axe'

More dancers have been axed from the line up of Strictly Come Dancing, in what is one of the most major shake ups it has ever seen - the Mirror takes a look at what is going on behind closed doors at the hit show

View 6 Images Six popular professionals have been dropped from the Strictly line-up (Image: BBC/PA Wire)

There's been a bloodbath over at Strictly Come Dancing with a slew of favourites being given the boot. When hosts Tess Daly and Claudia Winkleman announced last October they were leaving the dancing competition behind, it undoubtedly marked the end of an era for one of the UK's most popular TV shows - but now the BBC seem to be taking things even further.

‌



It's been dubbed a "fresh start" in some reports - but five professionals will not be returning to the our screens in a move that is said to have devastated the stars. There has been much drama behind the scenes of the nation's favourite dancing competition over the last few years, from the Giovanni Pernice 'bullying' row to Wynne Evans' lewd remarks and a former star being arrested on suspicion of rape. There is no suggestion that any of the axed dancers were involved in any wrongdoing.

‌



However, the shake up has been dubbed truly "brutal" by insiders, with those who have been sacked reportedly receiving very little warning about the major life change that was coming there way.

‌



A source told The Sun, "It's brutal. Once they're axed, there is very little in the way of duty of care and they are left to lick their wounds alone. Some of the dancers have been told their fate in a 15 minute phone call, even when they'd been on the show for years. Many of them feel like the BBC has pulled the rug from under them, with no warning."

Call The Midwife finale - everything you need to know about the last episode READ MORE :

View 6 Images Jamie Borthwick and Michelle Tsiakkas (Image: Getty Images)

Michelle Tsiakkas, 30, is said to have unceremoniously been dropped from the cast this year, after joining only three years ago. The relative newcomer wrote a cryptic post on her social media profile over the weekend. "Strength isn't about never breaking… it's about finding the light again, even after the storm," she said in a post for International Women's Day.

‌



In 2024, Michelle was paired up with actor Jamie Borthwick, but the Eastenders star got caught up in a storm of controversy after using a slur that is derogatory towards disabled people whilst on set of the show. He lost his job on the soap opera after the Beeb said his language was, "entirely unacceptable and in no way reflects the values or standards we hold and expect". Jamie, who had starred on Eastenders since 2006, apologised at the time for "any offence and upset".

Joining Michelle on the chopping block are Gorka Marquez, 35, Luba Mushtuk, 36 - both of whom have been part of the show for many years. Gorka even met his wife, Gemma Atkinson, on the dancing competition back in 2017. Luba has been part of the show since 2016, and has taken part in the some of the live tours across the UK, but is reported not to have been renewed.

View 6 Images Gorka Marquez and Luba Mushtuk are said to have been chopped from the line-up (Image: PA)

‌



Meanwhile, fan favourite Katya Jones, 36, was also rumoured to be facing the axe ahead of what is due to be her 10th year on the show. She was caught up in scandal back in 2018 when she was pictured kissing her celebrity partner, comedian Seann Walsh. He had a girlfriend at the time while she was married to long-term Strictly pro Neil Jones, who is not thought to be leaving. However, it was later claimed that bosses were 'desperate' to keep her because "they know how much viewers love her".

Sadly, her co-star Karen Hauer, 43 was not given a stay of execution. The long-standing pro - who continued to star on the show after her marriage to fellow pro Kevin Clifton collapsed - will not be back in September. Pals of Karen have told the Mail that they think the move to axe her is "disgusting," with an insider quoted as saying, "What message does it send?"

Nadiya Bychkova, 36 has also not had her contract renewed, with pals saying it "smacks of ageism" - but other insiders have claimed that this couldn't be further from the truth, and it has been drama behind the scenes that have caused the BBC to decide not to renew her contract. Nadiya's been left gutted by the decision, but its been claimed that the mother-of-one was "uncompromising" off screen and prone to tantrums.

‌



View 6 Images Karen Hauer will apparently not be returning (Image: PA)

Her breakup with fellow dancer Kai Widdrington is also said to have caused tensions on set, while her close friendship with BBC Breakfast journalist, Dan Walker, is also said to have become a "problem", according to the Mail.

They were paired together on Strictly back in 2021. Walker, 48, has been married to wife Sarah for more than two decades, but his enduring friendship with Nadiya has reportedly not been well received.

‌



"Nadiya's relationship with Dan still wrangles with many execs, it makes them feel uncomfortable and triggers needless attention," a source was quoted as saying. "It's not a great look for either of them, but as Nadiya is still connected with the show, she has more to lose by seemingly parading this cosy connection. Their friendship became a problem."

View 6 Images Katya with ex-husband Neil Jones (Image: BBC)

So what is the real story behind the drastic action? PR to the stars Mayah Riaz reveals that it's a typical brand response to controversy. However, the sheer scale of the cull suggests something much bigger is at play.

‌



"From a brand and comms perspective, decisions like this are rarely about just one incident or even one person. Every on-screen personality is effectively part of that brand ecosystem. So when the show goes through a period of controversy or reputational pressure, producers will start to look at having a wider reset rather than focusing on a particular talent.

"What I find interesting here is the scale of the changes," she explains. "When you see multiple professionals leaving at once, alongside the departure of long standing presenters, it suggests a broader repositioning rather than an isolated casting decisions. As this isn't just a routine casting tweak, but very much a brand reset."

View 6 Images Nadia finished fifth with Dan on Strictly 2021 and they remain close friends (Image: BBC)

‌



But how do popular stars like Nadia tie in with the 'reset'? Mayah believes bosses want a 'fresh start' with new dancers who are "not tied to any existing storyline or controversy."

Mayah explains, "Nadiya has been part of the show for a decade. Therefore, she has inevitably become associated with both positive and negative narratives around the show. Whether it's fair or not, producers sometimes decide that longevity means they carry some of that baggage with them.

"The reality is we have seen Strictly have a prolonged period of difficulty on their reputation front. Allegations around behaviour from some of the professional dancers generate headlines which shifts the narrative away from what the show is about - joy and escapism. By having a refresh of the cast can be a way to signal to viewers that the show is turning a page."

Article continues below

When it comes to why certain dancers were cut over others, Mayah insists that the decisions won't have been personal, and that the drama is cleverly designed to shift the narrative from past scandals. "You often see this in TV. It isn't really about punishing specific people but more about protecting the long term health of the format," she adds. "It seems that the producers feel the stories around the show has become dominated by backstage drama, relationship rumours, or complaints about conduct. And feel the quickest way they can reset the conversation is to have a shake up of the show - and we're seeing this is now what is being talked about.

"Strictly has always thrived on reinvention. By having new dancers, this brings a new fan base, new dynamics, and fresh social media interest. From a PR standpoint, it gives Strictly a chance to relaunch itself each season rather than feeling like a format that is ageing."

News.title: Donald Trump issues thinly-veiled fresh threat to Iran's new Supreme Leader
News.content:
Donald Trump issues thinly-veiled fresh threat to Iran's new Supreme Leader

Trump dismissed the war as a "little excursion" for the US, which had been "easier" than anticipated. And he played down skyrocketing fuel costs caused by the war, claiming oil prices would be "coming down" soon

View Image "We'll see what happens to them", Trump said cryptically (Image: Anadolu via Getty Images)

Donald Trump issued a thinly-veiled threat against Iran's new Supreme Leader - telling reporters: "We'll see what happens to them".

‌



He dismissed the war as a "little excursion" for the US, which had been "easier" than anticipated. And he played down skyrocketing fuel costs caused by the war, claiming oil prices would be "coming down" soon.

‌



Iran this week named Mojtaba Khamenei as its new Supreme Leader after a US air strike killed his father, the late Ayatollah Ali Khameni. Asked earlier whether he thought the US could declare a victory in the war with Khamenei remaining as Supreme Leader, Trump was unusually coy, saying he didn't want to comment. But later, during a factory tour in Cincinnati, Ohio, Trump issued a further threat to Iran's leaders.

‌



He said that the US had "knocked out twice their leadership" in Iran, and added: "Now they have a new group coming up. Let's see what happens to them."

"We did a little excursion," Trump said. "We had to take a couple of weeks, a few weeks of excursion, but it's been incredible, the military, the job they're doing is incredible. I would say, to put it mildly, way ahead of schedule."

‌



He went on: "We've knocked out their Navy, their military in all forms. We've knocked out just about everything there is, including their leadership, twice." Trump claimed the markets were "holding up well." He said: "I figured we would be hit a little bit, but we were hit less than I thought and we will be back on track in a pretty short while. Prices are coming down substantially, oil will be coming down."

Asked whether it was an "excursion" or a war, as he's previously described it, Trump said it was "both." It was, he said, an excursion for the United States and a war for Iran.

The US national average petrol price as of today is $3.578 a gallon - nearly 60 cents higher than it was a month ago, according to the US motoring and travel organisation AAA. The rising price of petrol is a major sticking point for Trump, whose "affordability tour" tonight reached Kentucky, where he will give a speech to a rally.

Article continues below

In November, the White House announced that Trump would travel the country more frequently in an attempt to stave off a drubbing in this year's midterm elections by showing he’s taking America's cost of living crisis seriously.

News.title: Coronation Street star 'lets slip' surprising murder suspect as she teases who dies
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Coronation Street star 'lets slip' surprising murder suspect as she teases who dies

Could Summer Spellman soon be a prime murder suspect? Actress Harriet Bibby reveals it "would not be a shock" if her character was to blame for next month's horror death on Corrie

View 6 Images Harriet Bibby, who plays Summer Spellman in Coronation Street (Image: Tom Dymond/REX/Shutterstock for NTA)

Mild mannered Summer Spellman is the last person Corrie fans would suspect of murder. But with her emotions running high, after a triple dose of grief in the nine years since she set foot on the cobbles, she could soon be a prime suspect. Harriet Bibby, who plays Summer, says: “I think if you push someone far enough, eventually they are going to snap”

‌



Summer moved to Coronation Street after her dad Drew Spellman died, having asked his ex, vicar Billy Mayhew, to raise her with Todd Grimshaw - who was then his partner. When Billy and Todd parted and the vicar fell for Paul Foreman, they became her ‘three dads’ until Paul died from motor neurone disease.

‌



Then Billy died in the recent Corriedale minibus crash, which Summer thinks was caused by Debbie Webster. In fact, her son Carl caused the crash, but viewers know that Theo Silverton fastened Billy’s seatbelt, so he was trapped when the van exploded. Now fans are on tenterhooks after the soap’s bombshell flash forward episode showed that a character - one of either Megan Walsh, Theo, Maggie Driscoll, Jodie Ramsay or Carl - will be murdered next month.

‌



Man spraying bus stations as pest control lands dream job on Coronation Street READ MORE :

If it’s Theo, Harriet says she will be a prime suspect - especially if she discovers the twisted scaffolder has been abusing his boyfriend Todd for months. She says: “So many things have happened to Summer. Is this the straw that breaks the camel’s back? It would not be a shock if it was.

View 6 Images Summer Spellman [HARRIET BIBBY] calls at the shop flat with a lovely photo of Billy and suggests to Todd Grimshaw [GARETH PIERCE] they display it at his funeral. (Image: ITV)

“The loss of Billy and the abuse of Todd are huge injustices. They’re down to one person and that’s Theo. So, if she found out, would she be as logical and empathetic as she has been in the past? I wonder if she would actually become quite angry and try to balance the scales of that injustice. It’s very possible that Summer would want to kill Theo. If Theo is the person that is murdered, then definitely, eyes would be on her.”

‌



Harriet joined Corrie in 2020, taking over the role of Summer from actress Matilda Freeman. At the time, Summer was 16 and Harrie was 21, which led to confusion when she tried to order drinks in pubs. She says: “I got asked for ID, but I still get ID-ed all the time now, I have to take it everywhere with me!

“I’m 27, but people tend to assume I’m younger than I am. An energy company knocked on my door the other day and when I opened it, they said: ‘oh, sorry, we need to speak to the owner of the house.’ I said ‘that’s me! I’m in charge of the bills.’ I think they were about to say ‘is your mum i?’”

Harriet grew up in Market Rasen, Lincolnshire, where she joined drama clubs and acted in school plays. She says: “It was always just a hobby. It was only when I was older and I did a summer school with the National Theatre that I heard people talk about drama schools.”

‌



6 View 6 Images Harriet Bibby attends the British Soap awards at Hackney Town Hall on May 31, 2025 in London (Image: Getty Images) 6 View 6 Images Tanisha Gorey and Harriet Bibby attend the "Corriedale" Premiere at The National Science and Media Museum on December 16, 2025 in Bradford (Image: Getty Images)

Studying at drama school in Wigan, Harriet then made her TV debut in an episode of Doctors in 2020. She received the life-changing call offering her the role of Summer the same year, while she was teaching a children’s drama class. She says: “I had to say ‘can I just nip out? I’m getting a really important call that I’ve been waiting for.’ Once I’d taken the call I went straight back in and finished the class before phoning my family to tell them I’d got the part. I had to keep my cool, but it was hard to concentrate.”

‌



Now she is stepping into unknown territory again - taking a break from the cobbles to star in a multi award-winning one-woman play, My Name Is Rachel Corrie. It tells the tragic story of young American activist Rachel, who travelled to Gaza in 2003 as part of an international peace brigade - living with Palestinian families, resisting the destruction of their homes. She died, aged 23, after being crushed by an Israeli armoured bulldozer.

Directed by Alan Rickman, the play premiered at London’s Royal Court Theatre in 2005 and has since been performed all over the world. Of the latest production at Manchester’s 53two theatre, Harriet says: “I’m on stage for an hour-and-a-half and there are 52 pages to learn. I’ve done theatre before, but not for years and I’ve never done a one-woman show before, so I was scared, but that made me want to do it more.”

Coronation Street writer Ian Kershaw invited her to take the role. She says: “He saw the play 20 years ago and caught me in work and asked if I’d be interested. It’s beautifully put together. It’s all Rachel’s own words from her diaries and emails home. A lot of the play is quite light-hearted. You are meeting this young woman who is curious, smart, weird and funny, but it’s also very poignant, so it’s emotionally draining and challenging too.”

‌



View 6 Images As Theo Silverton [JAMES CARTWRIGHT] shows Todd Grimshaw [GARETH PIERCE] the exercise bike he’s bought for him, a passing Billy Mayhew [DANIEL BROCKLEBAK] and Summer Spellman [HARRIET BIBBY] notice the cut on Todd’s face. Theo quickly explains that Todd injured himself whilst partying in London. Billy’s not convinced.

But Harriet, who lives in Manchester with her boyfriend Max, has a simple remedy to combat stress. She laughs: “Usually, when I’m at home, I’ll either be cleaning, sorting, or cooking. I find cleaning and pottering about quite therapeutic.” This sounds like the kind of stress-busting strategy viewers would expect from Summer, who Harriet shares several characteristics with.

“Summer always gets made fun of because she’s a bit of swot,” she smiles. “She tries really hard and she’s methodical and a bit geeky and I’m very similar. I have a colour-coded folder of my scripts that I take on set with me. People are like ‘you’re so Summer!’ And I say: ‘I know.’ Jodie Prenger [Glenda Shuttleworth] was taking the mick out of me on set the other day, because I said to her ‘what are you having for your tea?’ and she didn’t know. I said ‘what do you mean you don’t know? Do you not do a shopping list for the week and plan it out? I know what I’m having for the whole week.”

‌



Despite her Corrie demands, Harriet still spends Saturday mornings teaching at the same Manchester children’s musical theatre club that she worked at when she was a student. She says: “I’ve done it since 2018. The owner really looked after me before I joined Coronation Street and gave me lots of opportunities to teach, which kept me going. It keeps me busy and it’s fun and a nice release - it takes me out of things for a few hours.”

View 6 Images Summer Spellman [HARRIET BIBBY] and Nina Lucas [MOLLIE GALLAGHER] retrace their steps from the night before. Will they find the answers they’re looking for? (Image: ITV)

Life on the cobbles has been filled with drama for Summer. After helping Billy beat drug addiction, she has faced diabetes and bulimia. She suffered a miscarriage after falling pregnant at 17 and has experienced grief by the lorry load.

‌



Meanwhile, in real-life, Harriet was devastated by actor Daniel Brocklebank’s departure. She says: “He kept saying to me ‘I’m not actually dying, you can still see me.’ But it was upsetting, because since I joined the show, Dan’s been like my little guardian angel. He really did take me under his wing and we became good friends. We keep in touch regularly, but it was upsetting to know that I wasn’t going to see him every day in work.”

As for her dream role, Harriet would love a stab at Shakespeare’s Juliet. She laughs: “I’m 27, so I don’t know if I can wait much longer.” But, while she continues to be asked for ID in bars, anything is possible.

*My Name is Rachel Corrie runs from March 18 to March 22 at Manchester’s 53two Theatre. For tickets: www.ticketsource.co.uk

News.title: Popular sports retailer plunges into administration after 67 years with all web orders delayed
News.content:
Popular sports retailer plunges into administration after 67 years with all web orders delayed

A major sports retailer has called in administrators after 67 years of trading - and has told customers to expect delays on their online orders after a 'restructure of the business'

View Image The sports company has been trading for 67 years (Image: Getty Images)

A huge UK spotswear shop has entered administration - with customers all over the world without their orders. Running for 67 years, Quicks Archery, run by Archers was the go-to for archery equipment, parts, kits, books and accessories.

‌



It began as an archery deparment by David George Quick, and was located inside an already existing sports buisness called Hargreaves in Portsmouth. Ten years later, Mr George Quick was determined to make Quicks a dedicated independent archery specialist shop and branched out on his own.

‌



Quicks Archery in launched in 1949, and during the 50's and 60's the popular shop started booming after it became known for "The Bowman’s Guide" - a mail-order catalogue helping to standardise archery retail in the UK and garnered fans from all over the world.

‌



Major airline plunges into administration as all flights and bookings cancelled READ MORE :

In December 2019, the brand was incorporated into Quicks Archery Ltd, however it has undergone a significant transition since. A notice in The London Gazette was published on March 11, announcing the Holmfirth-based business had appointed Jonathan Paul Philmore of Philmore & Co Ltd as administrator on March 3.

As of Wednesday, Quicks Archery's official website has released a statement to all customers, explaining all website orders will be delayed. It reads: "Due to a restructure of the business, all orders placed via the website will be delayed. Thanks for your patience and understanding".

In 2024, according to Companies House, Quicks Archers faced a strike-off scare when it was nearly at risk of being shut down by the government.

‌



According to The Express, a First Gazette notice for compulsory strike-off was issued on November 12, which usually occurs when a company is late filing its accounts or if Companies House believes the company is no longer trading.

If this is successful, the company would have then been dissolved - yet this strike-off was suspended on November 21 and around a week later, the missing accounts had been filed and the compulsory strike-off action was discontinued, saving itself from dissolution.

The accounts show there were around 26 employees within the business, and operating as a medium-sized specialist retailer, with a payroll across three branches.

‌



It comes just under a year after the brand had taken a five-year lease on a state-of-the-art studio and shop in Leeds according to the West Leeds Dispatch. Quicks, together with its sister company Podium, also sponsored two archers representing Team GB at the Paris Olympics, Briony Pitman and Tom Hall.

Recently a major delivery firm which handled parcels across the UK has also gone bust with Evri shipments going missing. Pedal and Post has officially appointed liquidators after management shut down the ailing operation in January following nearly 14 years of trading. Brett Lee Barton and Margaret Carter, from Walsall-based BK Plus, have been named as joint liquidators.

News.title: Volcanic fragments rain down as Hawaii's Kilauea volcano erupts
News.content:
The US Geological Survey reported that this fountaining episode reached 1,300ft (400M). According to the USGS, Kilauea's plume sent tephra falling within Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park and into adjacent communities.

Tephra is glassy volcanic fragments made up of volcanic ash, pumice, scoria, Pele's hair and reticulate, created by lava fountains.

The heaviest tephra fall was recorded at Uēkahuna Overlook where about 4-7 inches accumulated.
